title: North England Regional Operations Report – March 2024
content:
File: 01_north_regional_ops_report.md
Title: North England Regional Operations Report – March 2024

# North England Regional Operations Report
**Period:** 1–31 March 2024  
**Prepared by:** Sarah Mitchell, Regional Operations Manager – North  
**Distribution:** Head of Operations, Supply Chain Lead, HR Business Partner

---

## Executive Summary

March has been a challenging month across the North England region. A combination of supplier delays, unresolved equipment faults, and ongoing staffing gaps has placed pressure on both the Leeds Central and Manchester Piccadilly branches. Customer satisfaction scores have dipped for the second consecutive month, and we are at risk of missing our Q1 service targets if corrective action is not taken before the end of April.

## Leeds Central Branch

Leeds Central continues to operate below capacity. The espresso machine on Bar 2 has now been intermittently failing for six weeks. The fault was first logged in mid-February — a pressure calibration issue that our in-house technician attempted to fix twice. Despite a parts order placed on 28 February, the replacement valve has not yet arrived. The machine is currently being managed by reducing output on busy periods, which is directly impacting throughput.

Separately, the branch received only 60% of its expected oat milk allocation in the first week of March. NorthBrew Supplies cited "logistics disruption at their Wakefield depot" as the cause. The shortfall meant we were unable to fulfil oat milk-based drinks for approximately two days. This was not communicated to customers in advance, which generated a number of in-store complaints.

**Current headcount:** 9 of 11 positions filled. Two barista roles remain vacant following the departures of Tom Okafor (resigned 14 February) and Priya Nair (transferred to Sheffield). Temporary cover arranged through agency staff, but continuity has been inconsistent.

## Manchester Piccadilly Branch

Manchester Piccadilly performed marginally better but is not without issues. The mobile ordering system, which went live on 4 March as part of the wider company rollout, experienced significant teething problems in the first fortnight. Orders were arriving with incorrect modifier data — several oat milk substitutions were not registering correctly in the POS system, leading to customer confusion and re-makes.

Two of the espresso machines at Manchester Piccadilly were flagged by the duty manager, James Rowley, as producing inconsistent extraction pressure. Engineering was contacted on 12 March and a site visit was scheduled for 19 March but subsequently rescheduled to 4 April. This delay is a concern, particularly heading into the Easter trading period.

## Supply Chain Summary

NorthBrew Supplies remains the primary concern. Oat milk deliveries have been unreliable for the past six weeks across both branches. Sarah Mitchell escalated formally to the supply chain team on 8 March. At time of writing, no resolution timeline has been confirmed.

## Actions Required

- Engineering to attend Manchester Piccadilly no later than 28 March  
- HR to fast-track Leeds Central barista recruitment  
- Supply chain to identify contingency oat milk supplier  
- Regional IT to investigate mobile ordering modifier sync issue  

---
*Report classification: Internal – Operational*

title: North England Regional Operations Report – March 2024
tags: [Leeds, Manchester, NorthBrew Supplies, oat milk, staffing, espresso machines]

title: Incident Report – Espresso Machine Failure, Leeds Central
content:
File: 02_incident_leeds_espresso_failure.md
Title: Incident Report – Espresso Machine Failure, Leeds Central

# Incident Report: Espresso Machine Failure
**Branch:** Leeds Central  
**Incident Ref:** INC-2024-0312  
**Date of Initial Fault:** 15 February 2024  
**Date of This Report:** 12 March 2024  
**Reported by:** Duty Manager, Leeds Central – Daniel Park  
**Escalated to:** Sarah Mitchell, Regional Operations Manager

---

## Incident Description

The commercial espresso machine on Bar 2 (a La Marzocca Linea PB, asset tag BP-LC-EM02) has been experiencing recurring pressure failures since approximately 15 February 2024. The fault manifests as inconsistent extraction pressure, typically dropping mid-shot, resulting in under-extracted espresso. During peak service hours this causes a significant backlog as baristas switch to the Bar 1 machine.

The fault was first reported internally by senior barista Chloe Barnes and logged in the branch maintenance journal on 17 February. Initial assessment by our in-house technician, Marcus Webb, identified a likely valve seal failure. A temporary fix was applied on 20 February; however, the fault recurred within 72 hours.

## Timeline of Events

| Date | Action |
|------|--------|
| 15 Feb | Fault first noticed during morning rush |
| 17 Feb | Logged in branch maintenance journal by Chloe Barnes |
| 20 Feb | Temporary repair by Marcus Webb (in-house technician) |
| 22 Feb | Fault recurs; machine placed on reduced operation |
| 28 Feb | Replacement valve ordered via BrewPulse procurement system |
| 5 Mar | Estimated delivery date missed; no update from supplier |
| 12 Mar | This incident report escalated to regional management |

## Operational Impact

With Bar 2 effectively running at reduced capacity, Leeds Central has been operating on a single full-pressure espresso machine during peak periods. Queue times during the 8–10am window have increased by an estimated 35–40%. Two customer complaints referencing slow service were received in the first week of March and have been logged separately in the CRM system.

The issue is compounded by the current staffing shortfall at Leeds Central (see regional ops report March 2024). With two barista positions vacant, the reduced-capacity setup is being managed by fewer experienced staff than is operationally desirable.

## Contributing Factors

It is worth noting that the oat milk supply disruption experienced in the first week of March (caused by a NorthBrew Supplies depot issue) further increased pressure on bar staff during the period when the machine was already underperforming. The combination of supply and equipment issues on the same days created a particularly difficult trading environment.

## Current Status

The replacement valve has not arrived as of the date of this report. Marcus Webb has sourced a compatible part from a third-party supplier as a contingency; cost approval pending from Sarah Mitchell. If approved, installation can take place within 48 hours.

## Recommended Actions

1. Approve emergency procurement of compatible valve part (estimated cost: £185 + VAT)  
2. Review preventative maintenance schedule for all La Marzocca units across North England  
3. Consider replacement of Bar 2 unit if further faults occur post-repair  
4. Escalate to procurement: recurring delivery failures for equipment parts must be reviewed  

---
*Incident classification: Operational – Equipment*

title: Incident Report – Espresso Machine Failure, Leeds Central
tags: [Leeds, espresso machine, equipment failure, Bar 2, maintenance, NorthBrew Supplies]

title: Supplier Update – NorthBrew Supplies Oat Milk Allocation Issue
content:
File: 03_supplier_northbrew_oat_milk.md
Title: Supplier Update – NorthBrew Supplies Oat Milk Allocation Issue

# Supplier Update: NorthBrew Supplies – Oat Milk Allocation Disruption
**Issued by:** Supply Chain Lead – Dominic Ferrara  
**Date:** 15 March 2024  
**Affected Branches:** Leeds Central, Manchester Piccadilly, Sheffield Meadowhall  
**Supplier Contact:** Kevin Holt, Account Manager, NorthBrew Supplies

---

## Background

NorthBrew Supplies has been BrewPulse Coffee's primary oat milk supplier for the North and Midlands regions since January 2023. Under the current contract, weekly deliveries are scheduled on Tuesdays for Leeds and Manchester, and Thursdays for Sheffield. The agreed minimum fulfilment rate is 90% of the ordered volume.

BrewPulse's oat milk consumption has increased significantly since the introduction of the oat flat white as a permanent menu item in October 2023. This has made the product a higher-risk line from a supply dependency perspective.

## Summary of Disruption

Beginning in the last week of February 2024, NorthBrew Supplies experienced a logistics disruption originating at their Wakefield distribution depot. According to Kevin Holt, the depot underwent an unplanned systems migration that caused routing errors in their outbound delivery scheduling. This resulted in partial and missed deliveries to multiple accounts, not solely BrewPulse.

The direct impact on BrewPulse branches was as follows:

- **Leeds Central:** 40% shortfall in week commencing 26 February; 15% shortfall in week commencing 4 March  
- **Manchester Piccadilly:** 30% shortfall in week commencing 4 March  
- **Sheffield Meadowhall:** Minimal impact; one delayed delivery resolved within 24 hours

Sarah Mitchell formally escalated the issue to Dominic Ferrara on 8 March, noting that the Leeds shortfall had already caused a service disruption (customers turned away from oat milk-based drinks). This is documented in INC-2024-0312 and the North England Regional Ops Report for March.

## Supplier Response

Kevin Holt provided a written apology on 11 March and confirmed the systems issue has been resolved as of 9 March. NorthBrew Supplies have committed to:

1. Full allocation resumption from week commencing 18 March  
2. A credit note of £420 against March invoices to compensate for the shortfall  
3. A formal incident report from their logistics team by 22 March

At time of writing, no formal incident report from NorthBrew has been received.

## Risk Assessment

Despite the supplier's assurances, the supply chain team has concerns about NorthBrew Supplies' infrastructure. This is the second disruption from this supplier in six months — a similar (smaller) shortfall occurred in September 2023, which was attributed to a haulage partner cancellation. At that time, no formal SLA review was triggered.

Given oat milk is now a high-volume, high-dependency product, Dominic Ferrara has recommended initiating a dual-sourcing review. A secondary supplier, GreenLeaf Provisions (South Yorkshire), has been identified as a potential backup. Commercial terms are under review.

## Actions Outstanding

- NorthBrew to provide formal logistics incident report by 22 March *(overdue as of report date)*  
- Supply chain to complete dual-sourcing assessment by 5 April  
- Contract review meeting with NorthBrew to be scheduled for April  
- Branches to maintain a minimum 3-day oat milk buffer stock going forward  

---
*Classification: Internal – Supply Chain*

title: Supplier Update – NorthBrew Supplies Oat Milk Allocation Issue
tags: [NorthBrew Supplies, oat milk, logistics, Wakefield depot, Leeds, Manchester, Sheffield, supply disruption]

title: Customer Feedback Summary – Leeds Central & Manchester Piccadilly, March 2024
content:
File: 04_customer_feedback_north.md
Title: Customer Feedback Summary – Leeds Central & Manchester Piccadilly, March 2024

# Customer Feedback Summary
**Branches:** Leeds Central / Manchester Piccadilly  
**Period:** 1–31 March 2024  
**Compiled by:** Customer Experience Team – Amara Osei  
**Source:** In-app feedback, Google Reviews, in-store complaint cards

---

## Overview

A total of 47 customer feedback submissions were received across the two branches in March 2024 — a 38% increase on February (34 submissions). Of these, 29 were classified as negative or mixed, representing a dissatisfaction rate of 61.7%. This is significantly above the company benchmark of 25%.

Both branches show similar complaint themes, suggesting systemic rather than branch-specific issues are driving negative sentiment.

---

## Leeds Central – Key Themes

**Volume received:** 26 submissions (18 negative, 6 mixed, 2 positive)

### 1. Drink Quality / Espresso Issues
Eleven customers specifically referenced weak or "watery" espresso. Language used includes phrases such as "flat taste," "not strong enough," and "not like usual." These complaints are concentrated in the 8–10am peak window and correlate directly with the ongoing Bar 2 machine failure documented in INC-2024-0312. Customers appear to be detecting the under-extraction issue even without awareness of the underlying equipment fault.

### 2. Oat Milk Unavailability
Six customers complained about being told oat milk was unavailable. Three of these referenced specific dates in the first week of March — consistent with the NorthBrew Supplies delivery shortfall. Two customers noted they were not warned in advance via the app or in-store signage, and one submitted a formal written complaint requesting a refund for a journey made specifically for an oat flat white.

### 3. Wait Times
Eight complaints cited excessive queuing, particularly during the morning rush. Several customers mentioned being told the "coffee machine was playing up." This is a transparency concern: while staff were trying to be helpful, informal references to equipment failures risk undermining customer confidence.

---

## Manchester Piccadilly – Key Themes

**Volume received:** 21 submissions (11 negative, 6 mixed, 4 positive)

### 1. Mobile Ordering Problems
Nine customers submitted feedback specifically about the mobile ordering experience. The most common issues: orders not ready on arrival, incorrect milk alternatives (particularly oat milk not applied despite being selected in-app), and one case of a duplicate charge. These issues map directly to the modifier sync problem identified in the March regional ops report — the POS integration bug that affected oat milk substitutions.

### 2. Staff Confusion / Communication
Four customers referenced staff appearing uncertain about mobile order procedures. One Google review described staff as "clearly undertrained on the new system." This is understandable given the app launched only four weeks before month-end, but it suggests the rollout support and training provision was insufficient.

### 3. Drink Quality
Four complaints about espresso quality, with similar language to Leeds ("bland," "weak"). Two of these were submitted on 18 and 19 March — the days following the rescheduled (and then delayed) engineering visit for the pressure issues on Manchester Piccadilly's machines.

---

## Cross-Branch Observations

The coincidence of oat milk shortages, espresso machine issues, and the mobile ordering rollout across the same period has amplified customer dissatisfaction beyond what any single issue would have caused in isolation. Customers experiencing a mobile ordering glitch alongside unavailable oat milk and slow service are significantly more likely to leave negative reviews.

Amara Osei recommends a coordinated response plan, including a proactive customer email for verified app users at both branches and a temporary service guarantee offer.

---
*Classification: Internal – Customer Experience*

title: Customer Feedback Summary – Leeds Central & Manchester Piccadilly, March 2024
tags: [customer complaints, Leeds, Manchester, oat milk, wait times, mobile ordering, espresso quality]

title: Staffing Issues Report – North Region, Q1 2024
content:
File: 05_staffing_issues_north.md
Title: Staffing Issues Report – North Region, Q1 2024

# Staffing Issues Report – North Region
**Period:** January–March 2024 (Q1)  
**Prepared by:** Sarah Mitchell, Regional Operations Manager – North  
**HR Business Partner:** Gemma Holroyd  
**Distribution:** Head of People, Regional Directors

---

## Summary

The North region ends Q1 2024 with a staffing gap that is wider than at any point in the previous 12 months. A combination of voluntary departures, an internal transfer, and slower-than-expected recruitment has left two branches — Leeds Central and Sheffield Meadowhall — operating below minimum staffing thresholds for extended periods.

This report outlines the current position, contributing factors, and recommended interventions.

---

## Leeds Central

Leeds Central currently has 9 of 11 budgeted positions filled. The two vacancies arose from:

- **Tom Okafor** (Barista, Grade 2) – resigned 14 February 2024. Tom cited personal reasons but informally mentioned dissatisfaction with shift patterns during his exit conversation with the branch manager. He had been with BrewPulse for 14 months.
- **Priya Nair** (Senior Barista) – transferred to Sheffield Meadowhall effective 3 March 2024 at her request, to reduce commute distance following a house move.

Both roles were posted on 19 February (Tom's replacement) and 4 March (following Priya's transfer). As of 28 March, no suitable candidates have progressed past first interview. One candidate withdrew after receiving a higher offer from a competitor.

Agency cover has been arranged through Apex Staffing Solutions. However, continuity has been inconsistent — three different agency workers have been placed in the past month, each requiring familiarisation time. The absence of an experienced senior barista is particularly felt during morning rush periods, where the Leeds Central setup requires two skilled operators at peak times. This has compounded the impact of the espresso machine issues on Bar 2 (see INC-2024-0312).

**Immediate risk:** If no permanent hire is made before Easter (29 March), the branch will enter its highest-volume trading period significantly understaffed.

---

## Sheffield Meadowhall

Sheffield received Priya Nair on transfer but was already carrying a vacancy of its own — a barista position left open since January following the departure of Marcus Eaton. Combined with Priya's arrival bringing one new starter into a busy environment, the Sheffield branch manager (Rosa Chen) has flagged concerns about induction capacity.

Priya is experienced but requires familiarisation with the Sheffield setup and has not yet been trained on the Sheffield Meadowhall-specific inventory system. Rosa Chen has requested a two-week overlap of support from a neighbouring branch, which is not currently possible given Leeds' own constraints.

---

## Contributing Factors

Sarah Mitchell has noted a pattern over Q1 that warrants attention at a structural level:

1. **Shift pattern dissatisfaction:** Two of the three departures in the region this quarter cited shift-related reasons (either formally or informally). The current rota at Leeds Central relies heavily on split shifts, which have been unpopular with younger staff.

2. **Pay competitiveness:** The competitor offer that led to the candidate withdrawal at Leeds Central was reportedly £0.75/hour above BrewPulse's current barista Grade 2 rate. Gemma Holroyd has flagged this to the People team for a market benchmarking review.

3. **Mobile ordering rollout pressure:** The introduction of the new ordering system in early March added training and operational complexity at exactly the time when branch teams were already stretched. Several staff members informally mentioned that the rollout felt rushed.

---

## Requested Actions

- Expedite Leeds Central recruitment; consider relaxing experience requirements or introducing a structured induction programme  
- Approve temporary uplift to agency contract for April to cover Easter peak  
- People team to complete barista pay benchmarking by 15 April  
- Review shift pattern model for Leeds Central with branch manager  
- Confirm whether cross-branch support for Sheffield can be arranged from Manchester or another North branch  

---
*Classification: Internal – People & Operations*

title: Staffing Issues Report – North Region, Q1 2024
tags: [staffing, Leeds, Sheffield, recruitment, turnover, agency staff, Sarah Mitchell, barista vacancies]

title: Maintenance Report – North England Branches, March 2024
content:
File: 06_maintenance_report_north.md
Title: Maintenance Report – North England Branches, March 2024

# Maintenance Report – North England Branches
**Period:** March 2024  
**Prepared by:** Marcus Webb, In-House Technician – North Region  
**Reviewed by:** Sarah Mitchell, Regional Operations Manager

---

## Overview

This report summarises all maintenance activity — scheduled preventative, reactive, and outstanding — for BrewPulse Coffee branches in the North England region during March 2024. Three branches are covered: Leeds Central, Manchester Piccadilly, and Sheffield Meadowhall.

---

## Leeds Central

### Reactive Maintenance – Espresso Machine Bar 2 (BP-LC-EM02)

This machine has been the primary maintenance concern in the region throughout February and March. Following the initial fault report in mid-February (logged formally as INC-2024-0312), I carried out two repair attempts:

- **20 February:** Replaced group head gaskets and recalibrated pressure profile. Machine returned to service.  
- **27 February:** Machine again reported under-pressure. Inspection confirmed valve seal failure — not addressable without replacement part.  
- **28 February:** Part ordered via BrewPulse procurement. Standard lead time quoted as 5–7 working days.

As of 12 March, the part has not arrived. I followed up with procurement on 6 March and was told the part is on back-order with the supplier. I have now sourced a compatible valve seal (non-OEM) from ProBev Engineering at a cost of £185 + VAT. Awaiting sign-off from Sarah Mitchell.

The machine is currently operational at reduced capacity. Bar 2 is being used for americanos and filter preparation where possible, with espresso-based drinks routed through Bar 1. This is not a sustainable arrangement for a location with Leeds Central's volume.

### Scheduled Maintenance – Bar 1 (BP-LC-EM01)
Routine descale and group head service completed 7 March. No faults identified. Machine in good working order.

### Grinder (BP-LC-GR01)
Burr inspection completed 14 March. Burrs showing wear but within acceptable limits. Flagged for replacement at next scheduled service (June 2024).

---

## Manchester Piccadilly

### Reactive Maintenance – Espresso Machines

Following concerns raised by duty manager James Rowley about inconsistent extraction pressure on two machines, I visited Manchester Piccadilly on **4 April** (originally scheduled 19 March but rescheduled due to a parts procurement delay — see Leeds section above, same visit was initially planned as a combined trip).

On inspection:

- **Bar 1 (BP-MP-EM01):** Pressure issue confirmed. The solenoid valve is showing early signs of failure — same class of fault as the Leeds Bar 2 machine. Temporary adjustment made; full repair requires the same valve part currently on back-order.  
- **Bar 2 (BP-MP-EM02):** No fault found on day of visit. Duty manager noted pressure issues had been intermittent. I suspect this may be linked to water hardness — Manchester's water supply sits near the upper threshold for scale accumulation, and the last descale was 5 months ago.

Descale scheduled for both Manchester Piccadilly machines within two weeks.

### Boiler Inspection
The commercial boiler serving the Manchester Piccadilly site hot water system was due its annual inspection in March. The visit was originally scheduled with ThermalPro Services for 25 March. This was postponed to 10 April due to engineer availability. This is a compliance-relevant item and should not be deferred further.

---

## Sheffield Meadowhall

No reactive maintenance issues in March. Routine scheduled service completed 11 March without findings. All equipment in good operational condition.

---

## Summary of Outstanding Items

| Item | Branch | Priority | Status |
|------|--------|----------|--------|
| Valve part procurement (OEM) | Leeds / Manchester | High | Back-ordered |
| Non-OEM valve installation (Bar 2) | Leeds Central | High | Pending approval |
| Solenoid valve repair | Manchester Piccadilly | High | Pending part |
| Boiler annual inspection | Manchester Piccadilly | Medium | Rescheduled to 10 Apr |
| Grinder burr replacement | Leeds Central | Low | Scheduled June 2024 |

---

## Note on Pattern of Failures

Both the Leeds Central and Manchester Piccadilly espresso machine faults are valve-related. These are La Marzocca Linea PB units of a similar age (installed 2019–2020). It may be worth reviewing whether the remaining Linea PB units across the network are approaching end-of-life for this component class. I can provide a full asset register with installation dates if useful.

---
*Classification: Internal – Facilities & Engineering*

title: Maintenance Report – North England Branches, March 2024
tags: [maintenance, espresso machines, La Marzocca, Leeds, Manchester, grinder, boiler, Marcus Webb, engineering visit]

title: Regional Performance Summary – North England Q1 2024
content:
File: 07_regional_performance_q1_north.md
Title: Regional Performance Summary – North England Q1 2024

# Regional Performance Summary – North England
**Quarter:** Q1 2024 (January–March)  
**Prepared by:** Sarah Mitchell, Regional Operations Manager – North  
**Reviewed by:** Head of Operations – BrewPulse Coffee  
**Date:** 5 April 2024

---

## Overview

Q1 2024 has been a below-target quarter for the North England region. While Sheffield Meadowhall maintained consistent performance, Leeds Central and Manchester Piccadilly both underperformed against their operational KPIs. The root causes are interconnected: supplier disruption, equipment failures, staffing gaps, and the complications of a mid-quarter technology rollout have combined to create a negative feedback loop across customer satisfaction, throughput, and staff morale.

---

## Branch-by-Branch Summary

### Leeds Central

- **Transactions vs target:** -8% (March alone: -13%)  
- **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSS):** 3.1/5 (Q4 2023: 3.8/5)  
- **Avg. queue time (peak):** 6.8 minutes (target: ≤4 minutes)  
- **Staffing level:** 82% of budgeted headcount throughout March  

Leeds Central's performance deteriorated through the quarter as the espresso machine fault on Bar 2 became a persistent issue. The transaction shortfall reflects both reduced throughput capacity and a number of customers not returning after poor experiences in early March. The CSS drop from 3.8 to 3.1 is the steepest quarterly decline recorded for this branch.

The customer feedback data (compiled by Amara Osei's team) identifies espresso quality and oat milk unavailability as the two most-cited drivers of dissatisfaction. Both trace back to supply and equipment failures that were known issues for weeks before their customer-facing impact peaked.

### Manchester Piccadilly

- **Transactions vs target:** -4% (mobile ordering offset partially recovered volume)  
- **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSS):** 3.4/5 (Q4 2023: 3.7/5)  
- **Mobile ordering adoption:** 23% of transactions by end of March (target: 20% — met)  
- **Mobile ordering complaint rate:** 18% of mobile orders generated a complaint or re-make in weeks 1–2  

Manchester Piccadilly tells a more nuanced story. The mobile ordering rollout achieved its adoption target — 23% of transactions by end of March — but the complaint rate in the first two weeks was uncomfortably high, at 18% of mobile orders. The modifier sync issue (oat milk substitutions not registering correctly in the POS) drove the majority of these. This was resolved by a software patch on 18 March, and complaint rates dropped to 4% in the final two weeks.

The espresso machine pressure issues at Manchester Piccadilly, while less severe than at Leeds, contributed to a modest CSS decline. The delayed engineering visit (rescheduled from 19 March to 4 April) meant the branch entered its final trading weeks of the quarter with unresolved equipment concerns.

### Sheffield Meadowhall

- **Transactions vs target:** +2%  
- **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSS):** 4.1/5 (Q4 2023: 3.9/5)  
- **Staffing level:** 95% of budgeted headcount  

Sheffield Meadowhall was the clear outlier in Q1. Stable equipment, consistent supply, and a well-embedded team (notwithstanding the vacancy carried from January) produced solid results. The branch was also the least affected by the NorthBrew Supplies oat milk disruption. Rosa Chen's operational management has been a positive factor — she proactively increased buffer stock in February following the September 2023 NorthBrew disruption, which meant Sheffield avoided the worst of the March shortfall.

---

## Regional Trends

**Supply chain:** The NorthBrew Supplies situation remains the most significant systemic risk. Two disruptions in six months affecting high-dependency products is not acceptable. Dominic Ferrara's dual-sourcing review must be concluded before Q2.

**Equipment:** The valve failure pattern across Leeds and Manchester Piccadilly La Marzocca units is a capital planning concern. If units of this age are approaching component failure at scale, the maintenance budget may need revision.

**Technology:** Mobile ordering rollout created short-term disruption but appears to be stabilising. Manchester Piccadilly's experience will inform the rollout schedule for remaining branches.

**Staffing:** The region cannot sustain sub-90% headcount across multiple branches simultaneously. Easter trading period (from 29 March) will test whether agency cover is adequate.

---

## Q2 Outlook

Recovery is achievable but dependent on resolving the outstanding equipment faults at Leeds and Manchester, completing the Leeds Central permanent hire, and stabilising the NorthBrew supply relationship (or activating an alternative). Sarah Mitchell has requested an additional £3,500 in discretionary budget for Q2 to cover agency staffing costs above standard allocation.

---
*Classification: Internal – Regional Performance*

title: Regional Performance Summary – North England Q1 2024
tags: [performance, Q1, Leeds, Manchester, Sheffield, KPIs, customer satisfaction, revenue, mobile ordering]

title: Logistics & Technology Disruption Report – Mobile Ordering Rollout, North Cohort
content:
File: 08_logistics_mobile_ordering_disruption.md
Title: Logistics & Technology Disruption Report – Mobile Ordering Rollout, North Cohort

# Logistics & Technology Disruption Report
**Subject:** Mobile Ordering Rollout – North Cohort / Concurrent Supply Chain Disruption  
**Prepared by:** Operations Integration Team – Lena Frost  
**Date:** 28 March 2024  
**Distribution:** Head of Operations, Regional Managers, IT Lead, Supply Chain Lead

---

## Context

This report documents the operational disruptions associated with the Phase 1 mobile ordering rollout in the North England region (Manchester Piccadilly — launched 4 March; Leeds Central — launched 11 March) alongside the concurrent NorthBrew Supplies logistics failure. The overlap of these two events created compounding difficulties that are worth capturing in a single document for the purposes of the post-implementation review.

---

## Mobile Ordering Rollout – Summary

The BrewPulse mobile ordering system (developed on the Orda platform, integrated with our Square POS) was launched in the North cohort as the second wave of a national rollout following a successful pilot in Bristol and Edinburgh in January 2024.

### Manchester Piccadilly (Launch: 4 March)

Go-live was technically successful. Within three days, adoption reached 11% of daily transactions. However, two operational issues emerged within the first week:

**Issue 1 – Modifier Sync Failure**  
A bug in the POS integration caused drink modifiers — specifically milk alternative selections — to not carry through correctly from the app to the kitchen/bar display. Oat milk substitutions were the most commonly affected. Staff were receiving orders showing standard milk despite customers having selected oat. This led to re-makes and, in several cases, customer complaints about allergen-adjacent errors (though no clinical allergen incidents occurred).

The IT team was notified on 7 March. A fix was deployed on 18 March. The two-week lag between identification and resolution was due to a dependency on the Orda platform's release cycle — the fix required a patch from the third-party vendor, not just a configuration change on our side.

**Issue 2 – Training Readiness**  
Staff at Manchester Piccadilly received two hours of system training on 1 March, three days before launch. In retrospect, this was insufficient given the concurrent equipment concerns and staffing pressures at the branch. Duty manager James Rowley noted in his weekly report that "the team are managing but it's a lot to absorb at once, especially with the machine issues still unresolved."

### Leeds Central (Launch: 11 March)

Leeds Central's launch was deliberately delayed by one week from the original plan (4 March) due to the branch's staffing situation and the ongoing Bar 2 machine fault. This was the correct decision. Even with the delay, the launch coincided with a difficult period — the oat milk shortfall had only partially resolved, and one of the two barista vacancies remained unfilled.

Mobile ordering adoption at Leeds Central reached 9% by end of March, below the 15% target for week three. Customer feedback suggests some users abandoned the app after experiencing the wait time issues in early March (related to equipment, not the app itself) and attributed the delay to the new ordering channel.

---

## Supply Chain Disruption – Interaction with Rollout

The NorthBrew Supplies oat milk shortfall (documented separately by Dominic Ferrara) directly worsened the mobile ordering experience at both branches. Because oat milk was unavailable for portions of the first two weeks, customers placing mobile orders for oat-based drinks arrived to find their order could not be fulfilled as submitted. This created a confusing situation where staff had to explain both the product unavailability and the new system's limitations simultaneously.

At Leeds Central, two customers who had placed mobile orders for oat flat whites during the shortfall period submitted formal complaints. One explicitly stated she believed the app had "taken her money for a drink they knew they couldn't make." Refunds were processed but the reputational impact (one complaint was posted on Google Reviews) was noted.

---

## Learnings and Recommendations

1. **Do not overlap technology rollouts with known operational stress periods.** The North cohort launched during an active supplier disruption and with branches carrying equipment faults. This should have triggered a rollout deferral.

2. **Modifier sync testing must include milk alternatives.** The oat milk bug was not caught in UAT because the test scripts did not include oat milk as a modifier combination. Test coverage must be expanded before the next wave (Midlands cohort, planned May 2024).

3. **Staff training should be minimum four hours** with a follow-up session one week post-launch. The Bristol/Edinburgh pilot used a four-hour model and reported significantly lower post-launch complaints.

4. **Real-time menu availability sync** — the app should reflect live stock status. If oat milk is unavailable, the menu item should be greyed out. This feature is on the product roadmap but not yet implemented.

5. **Supply chain status should be a go/no-go criterion for rollout.** If a branch is experiencing a confirmed supply disruption, launch should be paused.

---

## Status of North Cohort Rollout

As of 28 March, both Manchester Piccadilly and Leeds Central are live. The modifier bug is resolved. Adoption is trending upward. The recommendation is to proceed with the Midlands cohort in May 2024 but to apply the learnings above before launch.
